era_context: The immediate post-World War II period. Nazi Germany and Imperial Japan have surrendered; the United Nations is being established; the Soviet Union consolidates control over Eastern Europe; decolonisation movements accelerate across Asia and Africa; the atomic age begins with nuclear weapons as a defining geopolitical reality.

events.title: Nuremberg Trials conclude; verdicts and executions
events.description: The International Military Tribunal at Nuremberg delivered verdicts on 24 November 1945 and carried out executions on 16 October 1946. Twelve Nazi leaders, including Hermann Göring, Joachim von Ribbentrop, and Wilhelm Keitel, were executed by hanging. Three were acquitted; others received prison sentences. This trial established precedent for prosecuting crimes against humanity and war crimes, though it was criticised for victor's justice and the absence of Soviet crimes from the dock.

sources.name: International Military Tribunal, Official Records and Transcripts
Telford Taylor, The Anatomy of the Nuremberg Trials (1992)

events.certainty_note: Executions occurred on 16 October 1946; extensively documented in trial records and contemporary press.

events.title: United Nations General Assembly holds first session
events.region: London, United Kingdom (January); New York, USA (from September)
events.description: The first session of the United Nations General Assembly convened on 10 January 1946 in London with 51 member states. The Assembly elected Paul-Henri Spaak of Belgium as its first president and adopted its Rules of Procedure. In September, the Assembly relocated permanently to New York. This marked the formal establishment of the UN as the successor to the League of Nations, though Cold War tensions were already evident in debates over atomic energy and Soviet conduct in Eastern Europe.

sources.name: United Nations Official Records, General Assembly, First Session
Evan Luard, A History of the United Nations, Vol. 1 (1982)

events.certainty_note: First session convened 10 January 1946; extensively documented in UN archives.

events.title: Churchill delivers 'Iron Curtain' speech
events.description: On 5 March 1946, former British Prime Minister Winston Churchill delivered his famous speech at Westminster College in Fulton, Missouri, warning of Soviet expansionism and coining the phrase 'Iron Curtain' to describe the division of Europe. The speech articulated Western anxieties about Soviet intentions and is widely regarded as a symbolic opening statement of the Cold War, though tensions had been building since 1945.
key_figures: Harry S. Truman (US President, in attendance)

sources.name: Winston Churchill, Speech at Westminster College, Fulton, Missouri, 5 March 1946
David Reynolds (ed.), The Churchill Factor (2014)

events.certainty_note: Speech delivered 5 March 1946; text widely published and archived.

events.title: French Fourth Republic established
events.description: The Fourth Republic was formally established on 27 December 1946 when President Vincent Auriol signed the new constitution. This followed the rejection of a first constitutional draft in May 1946. The Fourth Republic restored parliamentary democracy but was marked by governmental instability, with 25 governments in 12 years. It inherited colonial conflicts in Indochina and Algeria that would ultimately lead to its collapse in 1958.

sources.name: French Constitutional Documents, 1946
Serge Berstein, The Republic of de Gaulle, 1944–1959 (1993)

events.certainty_note: Constitution signed 27 December 1946; documented in French state archives.

events.title: Philippines granted independence by United States
events.description: The Philippines were granted independence on 4 July 1946, ending 48 years of American colonial rule. Manuel Roxas became the first president of the independent Philippine Republic. However, independence was constrained by the Military Bases Agreement, which granted the US long-term control of military installations, and by economic ties that perpetuated American influence. The timing coincided with US decolonisation policy in the Pacific and reflected Cold War strategic interests.

sources.name: Philippine Independence Act, 1934; Proclamation of Independence, 4 July 1946
Stanley Karnow, In Our Image: America's Empire in the Philippines (1989)

events.certainty_note: Independence declared 4 July 1946; documented in US and Philippine state records.

events.title: Bikini Atoll nuclear tests (Operation Crossroads)
events.description: The United States conducted two nuclear weapons tests at Bikini Atoll in the Marshall Islands on 1 July (Test Able, air burst) and 25 July (Test Baker, underwater burst) 1946. These were the first nuclear weapons tests since Nagasaki and the first to be observed by international observers. The tests were intended to assess the effects of nuclear weapons on naval vessels and military equipment. The Baker test's underwater detonation created massive radioactive contamination, forcing the evacuation of the atoll's inhabitants and demonstrating the environmental hazards of nuclear weapons.
key_figures: William H. P. Blandy (Commander, Joint Task Force One)

sources.name: U.S. Department of Energy, Operation Crossroads: The Official Evaluation of the Atomic Tests at Bikini Atoll (1946)
Barton C. Hacker, Elements of Controversy: The Atomic Energy Commission and Radiation Safety in Nuclear Weapons Testing, 1947–1974 (1994)

events.certainty_note: Tests conducted 1 and 25 July 1946; extensively documented in military and scientific records.

events.title: Chinese Civil War resumes; Nationalist-Communist conflict escalates
events.description: After the Japanese surrender in August 1945, the uneasy alliance between the Chinese Nationalist (Kuomintang) government under Chiang Kai-shek and the Chinese Communist Party under Mao Zedong collapsed. By 1946, full-scale civil war had resumed. The Communists, controlling much of Manchuria and northern China, launched offensives that gradually eroded Nationalist positions. American mediation efforts under General George Marshall failed. By year's end, the military balance was shifting decisively toward the Communists, though the Nationalist collapse would not be complete until 1949.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Historian) — not applicable; contemporary Chinese military records and American diplomatic cables
Jonathan D. Spence, The Search for Modern China (1990)

events.certainty_note: Civil war resumed in 1946; extensively documented in Chinese, American, and Soviet archives.

events.title: Juan Perón elected President of Argentina
events.description: Juan Domingo Perón was elected President of Argentina on 24 February 1946 with 56% of the vote. His victory marked the beginning of Peronism, a populist movement that combined nationalist, labour-friendly, and authoritarian elements. Perón's coalition included the military, labour unions, and the Catholic Church. His wife, Eva Perón, became a powerful political figure. Peronism would dominate Argentine politics for decades and establish a model of populist authoritarianism that influenced Latin America.

sources.name: Argentine Electoral Records, 1946
Tulio Halperín Donghi, The Contemporary History of Latin America (1993)

events.certainty_note: Election held 24 February 1946; documented in Argentine state archives.

events.title: Enrico Fermi and team achieve controlled nuclear chain reaction (Chicago Pile-1 anniversary)
events.description: While the original controlled chain reaction occurred on 2 December 1942, 1946 marked the fourth anniversary and growing public awareness of nuclear energy's potential for civilian use. In 1946, the Atomic Energy Commission was established (replacing the Manhattan Engineer District), and serious discussions began about nuclear power generation. The year represents a symbolic transition from military secrecy to civilian nuclear development, though weapons research remained dominant.

sources.name: U.S. Atomic Energy Commission, Establishment and Early Reports, 1946
Richard Rhodes, The Making of the Atomic Bomb (1986)

events.certainty_note: AEC established in 1946; documented in government records.

events.title: British Mandate in Palestine deteriorates; Jewish insurgency intensifies
events.description: In 1946, the British Mandate in Palestine faced escalating violence from Jewish insurgent groups (Irgun, Lehi, and Haganah) demanding an end to restrictions on Jewish immigration and the establishment of a Jewish state. The King David Hotel bombing on 22 July 1946, carried out by Irgun, killed 91 people and marked a major escalation. The British responded with mass arrests and military operations. The situation became untenable for Britain, leading to the decision to withdraw and refer the matter to the United Nations, ultimately resulting in the 1947 Partition Plan.

sources.name: British Colonial Office Records, Palestine Mandate, 1946
Avi Shlaim, The Iron Wall: Israel and the Arab World (2000)

events.certainty_note: King David Hotel bombing occurred 22 July 1946; extensively documented.

events.title: Indochina War begins; French colonial forces clash with Việt Minh
events.description: The First Indochina War began in December 1946 when French colonial forces attacked Việt Minh positions in Hanoi and Haiphong, following the breakdown of negotiations between France and the Việt Minh under Hồ Chí Minh. The Việt Minh had declared independence in September 1945, but France sought to reassert colonial control. The war would last until 1954, becoming a major Cold War proxy conflict and a precursor to the Vietnam War. By 1946, the conflict was already consuming resources and lives.

sources.name: French Military Archives, Indochina Command, 1946
Martin Shipway, The Road to War: France and Vietnam, 1944–1947 (1996)

events.certainty_note: War began in December 1946; documented in French and Vietnamese sources.
cross_references: 1954-001 (Dien Bien Phu; end of First Indochina War)

events.title: Transjordan gains independence from British Mandate
events.description: Transjordan was granted independence on 25 May 1946, ending the British League of Nations Mandate. King Abdullah I became the ruler of the independent Hashemite Kingdom of Transjordan (renamed Jordan in 1949). However, independence was limited by the Anglo-Transjordanian Treaty, which granted Britain military bases and influence over foreign policy. Transjordan's Arab Legion, trained and officered by the British, would play a key role in the 1948 Arab-Israeli War.
key_figures: Glubb Pasha (British commander of Arab Legion)

sources.name: Treaty of Alliance between the United Kingdom and Transjordan, 22 March 1946
Avi Shlaim, Lion of Jordan: The Life of King Abdullah I (2007)

events.certainty_note: Independence declared 25 May 1946; documented in British and Transjordanian records.

events.title: Kennan's 'Long Telegram' shapes US Cold War strategy
events.description: On 22 February 1946, George F. Kennan, a US diplomat in Moscow, sent an 8,000-word telegram to the State Department outlining his analysis of Soviet ideology and expansionism. The 'Long Telegram' argued that Soviet communism was inherently expansionist and could only be countered by 'firm and vigilant containment.' This analysis became the intellectual foundation for the policy of containment that would define US Cold War strategy for decades. Kennan's ideas were later published as the 'X Article' in Foreign Affairs (1947).

sources.name: George F. Kennan, 'Long Telegram,' 22 February 1946, U.S. State Department Archives
John Lewis Gaddis, Strategies of Containment (1982)

events.certainty_note: Telegram sent 22 February 1946; archived in State Department records.

events.title: ENIAC (Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer) becomes operational
events.description: ENIAC, the first general-purpose electronic digital computer, became fully operational in February 1946 at the University of Pennsylvania. Developed during World War II for ballistic calculations, ENIAC weighed 30 tons, occupied 1,800 square feet, and consumed 150 kilowatts of electricity. Its successful operation marked the beginning of the electronic computing age, though its practical applications were initially limited to military and scientific calculations. ENIAC demonstrated the feasibility of electronic computation and inspired subsequent computer development.

sources.name: University of Pennsylvania, Moore School of Electrical Engineering, ENIAC Project Records, 1946
McCartney, Scott, ENIAC: The Triumphs and Tragedies of the World's First Computer (1999)

events.certainty_note: ENIAC became operational in February 1946; documented in university and military records.

events.title: League of Nations formally dissolved; assets transferred to United Nations
events.description: The League of Nations, founded in 1920 after World War I, formally dissolved on 18 April 1946. Its assets and archives were transferred to the United Nations, which had been established in October 1945. The League's failure to prevent World War II and its inability to enforce collective security had rendered it obsolete. The UN was intended to be a more effective successor, though it would face similar challenges in enforcing international law and preventing conflict.

sources.name: League of Nations, Final Records of the Assembly, 1946
Susan Pedersen, The Guardians: The League of Nations and the Crisis of Empire (2015)

events.certainty_note: League dissolved 18 April 1946; documented in League and UN records.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Nuremberg Trials has been contested by modern scholarship. While celebrated as establishing precedent for international justice, critics note that the trials were victor's justice: Soviet crimes (Katyn massacre, invasion of Poland) were excluded from prosecution, and the trials did not prevent subsequent atrocities. The trials' legal innovations (crimes against humanity) were not universally accepted at the time and remain contested in international law. Additionally, the claim that Nuremberg established a binding precedent for future prosecutions is undermined by the fact that subsequent international tribunals (Yugoslavia, Rwanda, International Criminal Court) required separate legal instruments and political will. The trials' effectiveness in deterring future war crimes is also disputed—many perpetrators escaped prosecution, and the trials did not prevent Cold War atrocities. Regarding the Philippines independence, the claim of 'independence' is complicated by the Military Bases Agreement, which granted the US effective control over Philippine military policy and territory, making the independence nominal rather than substantive in practice. The Bikini tests' environmental impact was initially downplayed by the US government; the full extent of radioactive contamination and health effects was not publicly acknowledged until decades later.
historiographic_note: 1946 is exceptionally well-documented due to the proximity to World War II and the beginning of the Cold War. Western sources (American, British, French) are abundant; Soviet sources are less accessible but increasingly available through declassified archives. Chinese sources on the Civil War are substantial but often reflect post-1949 Communist historiography. Coverage of decolonisation movements in Asia and Africa is less detailed in Western sources, reflecting the bias of contemporary documentation toward European and American perspectives. The year marks a transition from wartime to peacetime documentation, with a shift from military records to diplomatic and political archives. Indigenous and non-elite perspectives are underrepresented in surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Nuremberg verdicts and executions were the conclusion of trials that began in 1945
Churchill's Iron Curtain speech articulated the ideological basis for the Truman Doctrine
Escalation of Chinese Civil War in 1946 led to Communist victory in 1949
Deterioration of British Mandate in Palestine in 1946 led to UN Partition Plan in 1947
First Indochina War began in 1946 and ended with French defeat at Dien Bien Phu in 1954
Kennan's Long Telegram provided intellectual foundation for Truman Doctrine
